evidence_quotes.quote: This required connection will then cause the game to stop functioning entirely should the developer or IP holder pull their support from the game, rendering the product useless and worthless.
It should not be a small footnotek or hidden away inside a EULA.
for a fair use of a digital marketspace this is something that should've been presented with absolute clarity,
should not be allowed to self police and pressure companies to follow their own internal rules or morals of what products can be sold.

paragraphs.text: Hello, For a long time video games have been sold as a product that can be purchased either digitally or physically. These products have worked as detailed on the package/product page, and even after the developers of the IP stop providing active support to the game in question they have continued working if they have a mode for playing Singleplayer (playing alone without other people). In recent years this undestanding of what you buy digitally has changed. These games are now frequently made with a requirement for online connections, even when you play on Singleplayer, on a game saved locally to your computer that other people cannot access. This required connection will then cause the game to stop functioning entirely should the developer or IP holder pull their support from the game, rendering the product useless and worthless. Game have not been sold as a service, they have been sold as a product. For fair use of a digital marketspace this is something that should've been presented with absolute clarity, and so having a product you purchased taken away on the whim of a company seems extremely unfair, and I would also be willing to call it a scam. I am providing this feedback in connection with the European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Destroying Videogames", which garnered nearnly 1.5 million signatures by the time of me writing this. For a fair and transparent market the products or services sold need to be stated as such with absolute clarity, and for a product group that has recently decided to "swap" type this goes double. It should not be a small footnotek or hidden away inside a EULA. --- I would also like to touch upon the unfair practices of VISA and MasterCard from this month, where these companies have effectively taken the law into their own hands. Rather, they call themselves the law and enforce online traders to follow their rules instead of the laws of the country they are based in. Due to the massive market share these companies hold, this is akin to tyranny. Payment processing is more akin to a public service in the modern world, much like electicity and water, and is something the vast majority of people cannot survive without access to. If the company that stands behind the payments get to decide what they "like" and "dislike", and allow or decline service regardless of the laws of countries then there is no telling where they may go should such behaviour be allowed. For now the content unilaterally banned by VISA and MasterCard pertain to product with adult themes. It is primarily porn, but adjacent products that may broach similar themes as well. The old English proverb "give them an inch and they take a mile" may fit perfectly here. What can and cannot be purchased should be dictated by the country in which the transactions take place. If VISA or MasterCard wish to police this themselves, then they should also be ready to take responsibility for any illegal transaction done through their services. For the sake of fairness for both citizens and the companies I believe that VISA and MasterCard should not be allowed to self police and pressure companies to follow their own internal rules or morals of what products can be sold. Similarly, VISA and MasterCard will thus not be forced to bear the consequence should their service be used for illegal means where they realistically have no control.
